parsing_res_list.block_content: The Economist March gth 2024
Culture
Museum moves
What's in store?
Atmanyacclaimedinstitutions,a whole lot is never on view

B ENEATH THE gothic main hall of the Natural History Museum (NHM) in Londonis a labyrinthofcuriosities.Only atiny fraction of the museum's collection is on display-around 40,000objects out of some 8om. Much is consigned to the dim,crowded basement.Here is atortoise once owned by Charles Darwin;there are hundreds of jars of giant fish and thousands of rare shells.

"We've outgrown this space," says Clare Valentine,head of NHM's life-sciences collection. (In addition to the basement,the NHM also relies on storerooms in undisclosed sites.)Many museums have hoarding problem..The British Museum exhibits just 1% of its treasures; the Smithsonian National 1MuseumofNatural Historyin Washington,DC,only0.007%.In storagetreasures a usually protected,butthe old vaults are edangerously full.

One solution is E to move house. The NHM is undertaking the world's biggest relocation of a collection, as 28m objects are shifted toar new centre in Reading..Ifyou think packing up crockery and old photographs is tricky,imagine moving taxidermy:the bears,g giraffes and 面lions require fibreglass moulds to protect their fur,aswell as bespoke wooden frames.On arrival,they will be frozen for threedays 7beforebeing transferred into pristine,sanitised rooms.Ms Valentine reckons the whole move will take seven years.

seum and Ashmolean Museum have 国been ferrying objects to fresh buildings,too.In France curators at the National Museum of Natural History in Paris are grumbling over a plan to r move their collection to Dijon.Across the Atlantic, the [LIW Museum is in the final stages of shifting1.5m objects.

Moving may be stressful,but it can also afford the chance to rediscover, reassess and reorganise possessions. The Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York admitted that its curator of Islamic art had never seen its collection of Persian rugs because they are trapped at the back ofa storeroom.

And if no one knows what items are there,it can take a while to notice if some go missing. Last year it was revealed that a light-fingered curator had stolen up to 2,00o objects from the British Museum ands sold some of them on eBay. Wales's seven national museums are missing 2,00o artefacts;;the Imperial War Museum in London more ethan 500.
China's left-behind 国places  Hillbilly elegies

E PPO coin or potteryf fragment. Catalogues of goods are outdated and incomplete even at the most august museums. Teams employed to fill inventories are dwindling:the number of staff at the NHM has almost doubled in the past 50years,but the proportion caring for and researching collections has dropped from 55%to 15%.With the current staff,the NHM reckonsit would take 172 yearsto catalogueeverything. But a new team, brought in for the move,will create a detailed d database to register the relocated objects.
Modern buildings will shrink t to-do lists in other ways.At one museum in Britain,a curator spends half their t time check
Skeletons inthe closet
E

For some,ierere new storage fa cilities amount to window dressing.Museums are keeping too much,says Christopher Marinello,an art lawyer.He thinks the objects that are not on display should be donated dor sold.The Indianapolis Museum of Art used a ranking system to get rid of a fifth of its collection.Objects were given a score from "A"to "D"(denoting a scale from "rare" to "inferior")and stowed, sold or scrapped.

This sort of exercise could work for any collection.But it helps if objects are atractive in the first place.Selling a pickled fish is harder than a painting. For now,the priority for many museums is getting treasures to safer, more spacious ground.
BEIJING
Noirish books,films and TV shows depicting hardship are popular

TT DOES NOT sound like an easy place to T live.Scorched cars litter a desolate landscape. The city's factories are struggling;workers are being laid off in droves.Worst of all,a serial killeris sowing terror.

1Moses on 司the Plain",a novella of 2016by Shuang Xuetao,offersan unsparing portrait of life in China's industrial north-east in the 1990s.It inspired e film adaptation in 2021 and a television series in 2023.(It has also been translated into Englishin a collection called "Rouge Street".)In its various formats,the story is part of a phenomenon called r Dongbei renaissance".Dongbei isacollectiveterm forChina's rust belt:the provinces s of Heilongjiang,Jilin and Liaoning.The region,once knowni in n West as Manchuria,has become al byword for urban decay.Yet -ed cisely because of that,itis pushing to the popular culture.

The provinces were once the country's main manufacturing hub.In the 1950s a third of China's biggest industrial projects originated there;workers enjoyed job security pue good wages.Butin p 1980s economic reforms broke the region's -ow nopoly on production;state-run outfits downsized to make way for private firms.Mass redundancies followed in the 1990S.In 20018.3%of p north-east's labour force wasu unemployed.
Many Dongbei 国storytellers 1witnessed theser ruptures firsthand.MrS Shuang,Ban Yu and Zheng Zhi,the genre's most promi
